subject: GPS Constellation
executive_summary:
The Global Positioning System (GPS), operated by the U.S. Space Force, is the world's most widely used satellite navigation system, with 31-32 operational satellites providing positioning, navigation, and timing (PNT) services to billions of military and civilian users worldwide. The constellation is in the midst of a multi-decade modernization program, with GPS III satellites replacing aging Block IIF hardware and delivering improved anti-jam capability, enhanced civil signals (L1C, L2C, L5), and greater accuracy. As of March 2026, 83 GPS satellites have been built across all generations, with 32 launched and operational.

The GPS III program achieved a significant milestone with the launch of SV09 on January 27, 2026, aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket. SV10 has been declared Available For Launch but faces delay due to an investigation into the planned Vulcan Centaur launch vehicle. The next-generation GPS IIIF variant, featuring improved anti-jam capabilities and the first GPS-enabled search-and-rescue transponder, is expected to begin launching in late 2026 or early 2027. The long-delayed Operational Control System (OCX) Blocks 1 and 2 were finally delivered to the U.S. Space Force on July 1, 2025, a critical milestone for unlocking full GPS III capabilities.

GPS faces growing competition from China's BeiDou, Europe's Galileo, and Russia's GLONASS, all of which offer regional or global alternatives. The proliferation of alternative GNSS constellations reduces global dependence on GPS but also creates risks of signal interference and spoofing. GPS remains the gold standard for military PNT, but adversary development of GPS-denial capabilities — including jamming, spoofing, and anti-satellite weapons — represents the primary threat to the system's continued dominance.

key_findings.headline: GPS III SV09 Successfully Launched January 2026; SV10 Delayed
key_findings.detail: The ninth GPS III space vehicle (SV09) launched successfully on January 27, 2026, from Cape Canaveral aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9. SV10 has completed construction and been declared Available For Launch, but its launch date is uncertain due to an investigation into the planned United Launch Alliance Vulcan Centaur rocket. The GPS III series delivers 3x better accuracy and 8x improved anti-jam capability compared to Block IIF satellites.

key_findings.headline: GPS IIIF Next-Generation Satellites Expected to Launch Late 2026
key_findings.detail: The first GPS IIIF satellites are entering the integration stage of production, with initial launch expected in late 2026 or early 2027. IIIF satellites feature improved anti-jam capability, a new search-and-rescue payload, enhanced civil signal (L1C), and a regional military signal (RMS). The IIIF program represents the most significant GPS upgrade in a decade, positioning the constellation against competing systems like BeiDou-3 and Galileo Second Generation.

key_findings.headline: OCX Ground Control System Finally Delivered After Years of Delays
key_findings.detail: The Next Generation Operational Control System (OCX) Blocks 1 and 2 were delivered to the U.S. Space Force on July 1, 2025, after years of delays and cost overruns documented by the GAO. OCX is essential for unlocking the full capabilities of GPS III satellites, including the M-code military signal and L1C civil signal. OCX Block 3F, needed for IIIF satellite control, is scheduled for 2026 delivery.

key_findings.headline: 32 Operational Satellites Exceed Minimum Constellation Requirement
key_findings.detail: The GPS constellation currently operates 32 satellites, exceeding the 24-satellite minimum required for global coverage. Typically 31 are operational at any given time. However, several older Block IIF satellites are approaching end-of-life, making the GPS III and IIIF deployment cadence critical for maintaining constellation health. The constellation provides sub-meter accuracy for military users with M-code and 3-5 meter accuracy for civilian users.

key_findings.headline: Growing GPS-Denial Threat from Adversary Jamming and Spoofing
key_findings.detail: Adversary development of GPS-denial capabilities represents the most significant threat to the system. Russian forces have extensively deployed GPS jamming in Ukraine, while China has invested in GPS-denial capabilities for its A2/AD strategy in the Western Pacific. The improved anti-jam capability of GPS III (8x over Block IIF) and IIIF satellites is a direct response, but the proliferation of low-cost jamming and spoofing technology means the threat is outpacing defensive upgrades.

key_findings.headline: Competing GNSS Constellations Reduce Global GPS Dependence
key_findings.detail: GPS now competes with three other global navigation systems: China's BeiDou (50 satellites, global coverage), Europe's Galileo (26 operational), and Russia's GLONASS (24 operational). BeiDou has surpassed GPS in total satellite count and is integrated into over 2 billion devices globally. While multi-GNSS receivers provide redundancy, the proliferation of alternatives gives adversaries options to operate independently of GPS, reducing U.S. PNT leverage.

recent_developments.headline: GPS III SV09 Launches Successfully on SpaceX Falcon 9
recent_developments.summary: Lockheed Martin's ninth GPS III space vehicle launched on January 27, 2026, from Cape Canaveral Space Force Station aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket. SV09 joins the operational constellation, bringing the latest anti-jam, accuracy, and signal capabilities to the fleet. This was the ninth of ten planned GPS III satellites.
recent_developments.impact: Continues the modernization of the GPS constellation with improved military and civilian capabilities, maintaining the deployment cadence needed to replace aging Block IIF satellites.

recent_developments.headline: OCX Ground Control System Blocks 1 and 2 Delivered to Space Force
recent_developments.summary: The Next Generation Operational Control System (OCX), developed by Raytheon, was delivered to the U.S. Space Force on July 1, 2025, after years of delays and billions in cost overruns. OCX is essential for commanding GPS III satellites and enabling advanced features including the M-code military signal. The GAO had repeatedly flagged OCX delays as a risk to GPS modernization.
recent_developments.impact: Unlocks the full capability of GPS III satellites already in orbit, particularly the encrypted M-code signal critical for military PNT in contested environments.

recent_developments.headline: GPS IIIF Satellites Enter Integration Stage
recent_developments.summary: The first GPS IIIF satellites have entered the integration stage of production at Lockheed Martin's facilities, with the initial launch planned for late 2026 or early 2027. IIIF represents the most significant GPS upgrade in over a decade, adding a search-and-rescue payload, regional military signal, and enhanced anti-jam capability. Six IIIF satellites are under contract.
recent_developments.impact: Positions GPS to remain competitive against BeiDou-3 and Galileo Second Generation, both of which are deploying enhanced capabilities in the same timeframe.

risk_assessment.current_risk: Moderate. The GPS constellation is healthy with 32 operational satellites exceeding the 24-satellite minimum. Modernization is progressing with GPS III deployments and OCX delivery. However, aging Block IIF satellites, IIIF production timelines, adversary GPS-denial capabilities, and growing competition from BeiDou and Galileo present ongoing challenges.
key_drivers: Adversary GPS-denial capabilities (jamming, spoofing, ASAT) growing faster than defensive upgrades
BeiDou achieving parity or superiority in satellite count and regional accuracy
GPS IIIF launch schedule dependent on Vulcan Centaur vehicle readiness
Aging Block IIF satellites approaching end-of-life requiring sustained replacement cadence
OCX ground system still completing capability rollout, limiting full GPS III utilization
mitigation_actions: Maintain GPS III/IIIF launch cadence to ensure timely replacement of aging Block IIF satellites
Accelerate M-code receiver fielding across DoD platforms for improved anti-jam PNT
Invest in alternative PNT technologies (LEO PNT, inertial navigation, timing) to reduce GPS dependence
Complete OCX Block 3F delivery on schedule to support IIIF satellite operations
Strengthen international partnerships for GNSS interoperability and signal monitoring
